# Rust apps running within Quarto static websites hosted on Github Pages

Sound magic, right?

- The speed and safety from Rust, gracefully provided via binary compiling, strong type-setting, and memory allocation
- The easy of making website from Quarto, provided by Pandoc
- The ease of hosting websites and automatically updating them through Github Pages CI

**But is this even possible?**

TLDR; *it is* 🎉🥳

Here's a "hallo-leptos" script sources from a quarto (.qmd) file: 

<iframe
  src="/apps/hallo-leptos/index.html"
  width="100%"
  height="300"
  style="border: none;">
</iframe>

## The guide:

### 1) Make a leptos csr (Client-side rendering) using `Trunk`.

Continuously monitor the progress using
 
 ```{.zsh filename="~/hallo-leptos/"}
trunk serve --open
 ```

When the app is ready for deployment, run:

 ```{.zsh filename="~/hallo-leptos/"}
 trunk build --release 
 ```

[link to leptos guide](https://book.leptos.dev/getting_started/index.html) for a deeper explanation.

### 2) Ingest the leptos app in our Quarto webpage setup

First, make an `apps` folder and include all apps here, e.g.:

 ```{.zsh filename="~/my-quarto-website-root/"}
 mkdir apps
 cd apps
 mkdir hallo-leptos
 ```

 now, copy-past the files from `/dist` in our leptos project into the apps folder within the subfolder `hallo-leptos`.

### 3) Adjust quarto project setup

go to the project roots `_quarto.yml` and add:

``` markdown filename="~/my-quarto-website-root/_quarto.yml"
resources: 
 - apps/**
```

Because the leptos app initially made a html with a directory without Quarto in mind, now go a correct the dir:

```{.html filename="~/my-quarto-website-root/apps/hallo-leptos/index.html"} 
/leptos-tutorial_101-68a2266496ae0b94.js
```

should be:

```{.html filename="~/my-quarto-website-root/apps/hallo-leptos/index.html"}
/apps/hallo-leptos/leptos-tutorial_101-68a2266496ae0b94.js
```

and 

```{.html filename="~/my-quarto-website-root/apps/hallo-leptos/index.html"} 
'/leptos-tutorial_101-68a2266496ae0b94_bg.wasm'
```

will become:

```{.html filename="~/my-quarto-website-root/apps/hallo-leptos/index.html"}
'/apps/hallo-leptos/leptos-tutorial_101-68a2266496ae0b94_bg.wasm'
```

*note: your file names will have different names*

### 4) Embed the leptos html app in a .qmd file

Finally, we can go to a page which we want to embed our leptos html in.

You can either:

``` {.markdown filename="~/my-quarto-website-root/some-folder/specific-page.qmd"}
[Open app](/apps/hallo-leptos/index.html)
```

or use `iframe`

``` {.markdown filename="~/my-quarto-website-root/some-folder/specific-page.qmd"}
<iframe
  src="/apps/hallo-leptos/index.html"
  width="100%"
  height="300"
  style="border: none;">
</iframe>
```


<!--
![](apps/hallo-leptos/index.html){width="100%" height="600px"}
-->

This will produce the following:

[Open app](/apps/hallo-leptos/index.html)

<iframe
  src="/apps/hallo-leptos/index.html"
  width="100%"
  height="300"
  style="border: none;">
</iframe>

## Caveats

While this is amazing and exciting, I'm still puzzled by some issues:

### 1) quarto preview doesn't work with the leptos .html

One of the amazing things about Quarto is it's ease and quick `quarto preview` CLI functionality. 

Unfortunately, this doesn't work with the leptos .html. In fact, opening the `ìndex.html` in the browser (directly from the file) will also simply render a blank page.

After doing some reading, it seems that the leptos framework require a secure http url. 

Quarto only hosts locally (local port) and opening the file directly opens a `file:` url. Maybe there's a trick to solve this?
